disco.ex: My parents went to a disco last Saturday night and danced to old pop songs.

discomfort.label: CEFR B1
discomfort.def: A feeling of slight pain or physical unease, or a sense of worry or embarrassment.
discomfort.ex: She felt some discomfort in her back after sitting for hours at her desk.

disconnect.label: CEFR B2
disconnect.def: To break a connection or link between things, or to stop being involved with something.
disconnect.ex: After the meeting, she decided to disconnect from social media to focus on her work.

discount.label: CEFR B1
discount.def: A reduction in the usual price of something, so you pay less.
discount.ex: The shop is offering a 20% discount on all winter clothes this week.

discourage.label: CEFR B1
discourage.def: To make someone less confident or enthusiastic about doing something.
discourage.ex: The bad weather didn't discourage us from going on our camping trip.

discover.label: CEFR A2
discover.def: To find or learn something for the first time.
discover.ex: When I visited London, I discovered a beautiful café near the museum.

discovery.label: CEFR B1
discovery.def: Finding or learning about something for the first time that was previously unknown or hidden.
discovery.ex: Marie Curie's discovery of radium changed the field of chemistry forever.

discredit.label: CEFR B2
discredit.def: To damage someone's reputation or make people doubt their credibility and trustworthiness.
discredit.ex: The leaked emails discredited the politician, leading many voters to question his integrity.

discriminate.label: CEFR B2
discriminate.def: To treat a person or group unfairly based on characteristics like race, gender, or age.
discriminate.ex: Companies that discriminate against job applicants based on their appearance face legal consequences.

discrimination.label: CEFR B1
discrimination.def: Unfair treatment of a person or group based on their identity, such as race, gender, or age.
discrimination.ex: The company was fined for discrimination against female workers who earned less than men in similar jobs.

discuss.label: CEFR A1
discuss.def: To talk about something with another person, sharing ideas and opinions.
discuss.ex: We discussed our holiday plans at dinner last night.

discussion.label: CEFR A2
discussion.def: A conversation where people talk about a topic and share different ideas.
discussion.ex: We had a discussion about the best way to solve the problem.

disease.label: CEFR B1
disease.def: An illness or health condition that affects the body or mind negatively
disease.ex: Travellers should get vaccinated against tropical diseases before visiting Africa.

disgrace.label: CEFR B2
disgrace.def: Loss of respect or reputation caused by shameful or dishonourable behaviour.
disgrace.ex: The politician's corruption scandal was considered a disgrace to the entire nation.

disguise.label: CEFR B2
disguise.def: To change your appearance or conceal your identity so others cannot recognise you.
disguise.ex: The spy disguised herself as a cleaner to gain access to the building without being detected.

disgusting.label: CEFR B1
disgusting.def: extremely unpleasant or offensive; making you feel sick or angry
disgusting.ex: The restaurant's kitchen was in a disgusting state, so we complained to the manager.

dish.label: CEFR A1
dish.def: A flat plate used for holding or serving food
dish.ex: I put the clean dishes away in the cupboard.

dishonest.label: CEFR A2
dishonest.def: Not telling the truth; trying to deceive or cheat someone
dishonest.ex: It was dishonest of him to say he finished his homework when he didn't.

dishonesty.label: CEFR B2
dishonesty.def: The act of being untruthful or deliberately deceptive for personal gain.
dishonesty.ex: The company's dishonesty about product safety led to a major scandal and loss of customer trust.

dishwasher.label: CEFR B1
dishwasher.def: A machine that washes dishes, glasses, and cutlery automatically using hot water and detergent.
dishwasher.ex: After dinner, I put the plates and cups in the dishwasher instead of washing them by hand.

disillusion.label: CEFR B2
disillusion.def: To cause someone to lose their belief that something is good or true.
disillusion.ex: The scandal disillusioned many voters who had previously supported the government.

disk/disc.label: CEFR B1
disk/disc.def: A flat, round object used to store or play music, videos, or computer data.
disk/disc.ex: She inserted a disc into her laptop to install the software.

dislike.label: CEFR B1
dislike.def: To not enjoy something or feel unhappy about it.
dislike.ex: Many people dislike waiting in long queues at the supermarket.

dismiss.label: CEFR B2
dismiss.def: To tell someone they can leave, or to reject an idea as unimportant or unworthy of consideration
dismiss.ex: The judge dismissed the case due to insufficient evidence, allowing the defendant to walk free.

disorder.label: CEFR B2
disorder.def: A condition where something is not organised or where a physical or mental problem exists
disorder.ex: The doctor diagnosed him with an anxiety disorder that required professional treatment.

disorderly.label: CEFR B2
disorderly.def: Lacking organisation or control; chaotic and untidy in behaviour or appearance.
disorderly.ex: The disorderly crowd at the concert forced police to intervene and restore calm.

disorganized/disorganised.label: CEFR B2
disorganized/disorganised.def: Not arranged in a logical order; lacking clear planning or system in how things are structured.
disorganized/disorganised.ex: The disorganised filing system made it difficult for the team to locate important documents quickly.

dispensable.label: CEFR B2
dispensable.def: Not essential; able to be removed or done without without causing major problems
dispensable.ex: Some people argue that single-use plastics are dispensable, but others believe they serve an important purpose in modern life.

displace.label: CEFR B2
displace.def: To force someone or something to leave their home or original position.
displace.ex: The flooding displaced thousands of families who had to seek shelter in nearby towns.

displeasure.label: CEFR B2
displeasure.def: A feeling of dissatisfaction, annoyance, or disapproval about something or someone.
displeasure.ex: The minister's displeasure at the leaked documents was evident during the press conference.

disposal.label: CEFR B2
disposal.def: The action or process of getting rid of something, especially waste or unwanted materials.
disposal.ex: The company faced strict regulations regarding the disposal of hazardous chemicals from their manufacturing plant.

disposition.label: Oxford 5000 · C1
disposition.def: A person's natural tendency to behave or think in a particular way; temperament or attitude.
disposition.ex: Her cheerful disposition made her popular with colleagues, even during stressful projects at work.

dispute.label: Oxford 5000 · C1
dispute.def: A disagreement or argument between people or groups about something important.
dispute.ex: The two countries have been involved in a long-running dispute over the territorial boundaries.

disqualify.label: CEFR B2
disqualify.def: To declare someone officially unable to compete or hold a position due to breaking rules.
disqualify.ex: The athlete was disqualified from the competition for using a prohibited substance.

disregard.label: CEFR B2
disregard.def: A failure or refusal to pay attention to something or treat it as important
disregard.ex: The company's disregard for safety regulations led to serious consequences for its workers.

disrupt.label: CEFR B2
disrupt.def: To interrupt or disturb the normal progress or order of something
disrupt.ex: The unexpected strike disrupted public transport services throughout the city for several days.

dissatisfaction.label: CEFR B2
dissatisfaction.def: A feeling of not being happy or content with something or someone.
dissatisfaction.ex: Growing dissatisfaction with the company's management led many employees to seek positions elsewhere.

dissatisfied.label: CEFR B2
dissatisfied.def: Not happy or pleased with something; wanting something to be different or better
dissatisfied.ex: Many customers were dissatisfied with the quality of service at the restaurant and complained to the manager.

dissident.label: CEFR B2
dissident.def: A person who publicly disagrees with and opposes the government or an official organization.
dissident.ex: The political dissident was arrested for criticizing the regime's human rights record.

dissolve.label: CEFR B1
dissolve.def: To mix a solid into liquid until it disappears, or to end an agreement or organisation
dissolve.ex: You need to dissolve the sugar in hot water before making the lemonade.

distance.label: CEFR B1
distance.def: The space or length between two places or things.
distance.ex: The distance between London and Manchester is about 200 miles.

distant.label: CEFR B1
distant.def: Far away in space or time; not close or near
distant.ex: She could see the distant mountains through the morning fog.

distinction.label: CEFR B1
distinction.def: A clear difference between two things or people that makes them special or separate
distinction.ex: There is an important distinction between working part-time and working full-time.

distinctly.label: CEFR B1
distinctly.def: In a way that is clear and easy to notice or understand
distinctly.ex: I distinctly remember she wore a red dress at the party last night.

distinguish.label: CEFR B1
distinguish.def: To recognize or understand the difference between two things
distinguish.ex: It can be difficult to distinguish between similar twins when you first meet them.

distinguished.label: CEFR B2
distinguished.def: Widely recognised and respected for outstanding achievements or qualities.
distinguished.ex: The distinguished professor was invited to speak at universities around the world about her groundbreaking research.

distortion.label: CEFR B2
distortion.def: A change from the original state or form; a false or misleading representation of something
distortion.ex: The reporter was accused of presenting a distortion of the facts to support her argument.

distract.label: CEFR B2
distract.def: To interrupt someone's attention or concentration on something
distract.ex: The loud construction noise outside distracted me from completing my important assignment.

distraction.label: CEFR B2
distraction.def: Something that draws your attention away from what you're trying to focus on or do.
distraction.ex: The constant notifications on her phone were a major distraction during the exam.

distressing.label: CEFR B2
distressing.def: Causing worry, sadness, or emotional pain to someone
distressing.ex: The distressing news about the accident left families searching for information at the hospital.

distribution.label: CEFR B1
distribution.def: The way something is shared or spread out across different places or among people.
distribution.ex: The distribution of food to remote villages was delayed due to bad weather.

distributor.label: CEFR B2
distributor.def: A company or person that supplies goods from manufacturers to retailers or customers.
distributor.ex: The automotive distributor ensures that spare parts reach dealerships across the country within 48 hours.

district.label: CEFR B1
district.def: A specific area or region of a city, country, or other place with its own characteristics.
district.ex: The historic district of the old town attracts thousands of tourists every year.

disturb.label: CEFR A2
disturb.def: To interrupt someone or make it hard for them to concentrate or rest.
disturb.ex: Please don't disturb me while I'm studying for my exam.

disturbance.label: CEFR B1
disturbance.def: Something that interrupts or upsets the normal, peaceful state of a place or person.
disturbance.ex: The loud music from the party next door was a real disturbance to the neighbours trying to sleep.

dive.label: CEFR B1
dive.def: To jump into water headfirst, or to move downward quickly through the air or water.
dive.ex: The Olympic athlete performed a beautiful dive from the ten-metre platform.

diver.label: CEFR B1
diver.def: A person who swims underwater, either for sport or to explore beneath the water surface.
diver.ex: The professional diver explored the coral reef and photographed many colourful fish.

diversion.label: CEFR B2
diversion.def: A change of direction or route, or something that takes attention away from the main issue.
diversion.ex: The roadworks created a traffic diversion that added twenty minutes to my commute.

divert.label: CEFR B2
divert.def: To change the direction or course of something, or to redirect attention elsewhere
divert.ex: The government decided to divert funds from defence spending to improve the healthcare system.

divide.label: CEFR A2
divide.def: To separate something into parts or groups
divide.ex: The teacher divided the class into two teams for the game.

divided.label: CEFR B2
divided.def: Split into different parts or groups; not united in opinion or feeling.
divided.ex: The committee remained divided on whether to approve the new policy, with half supporting it and half opposing it.

divine.label: CEFR B1
divine.def: Connected to God or gods; holy and sacred
divine.ex: Many people believe that nature's beauty is divine and inspires spiritual feelings.

diving.label: CEFR B1
diving.def: Sport where you jump into water from a high place, performing tricks in the air.
diving.ex: She took up diving lessons at the local swimming pool and loves trying new moves.

division.label: CEFR B2
division.def: The act of separating something into parts or groups; disagreement between people or groups.
division.ex: There is a clear division between the wealthy and poor neighbourhoods in the city.

divorce.label: CEFR B2
divorce.def: To legally end a marriage by court decision.
divorce.ex: After twenty years together, they decided to divorce and go their separate ways.

divorced.label: CEFR B1
divorced.def: No longer married to someone after a legal separation
divorced.ex: After five years of marriage, they became divorced and each started new lives.

dizzy.label: CEFR A2
dizzy.def: Feeling as if the room is spinning; unsteady and confused about position.
dizzy.ex: After spinning around quickly, Sarah felt dizzy and had to sit down.

dj.label: CEFR B1
dj.def: A person who plays recorded music at events, clubs, or on radio stations for entertainment.
dj.ex: The DJ at the wedding played all the songs the bride and groom had requested.

dna.label: CEFR B2
dna.def: A molecule in living cells that carries genetic instructions inherited from parents and determines biological characteristics.
dna.ex: Scientists analysed the DNA from the crime scene to identify the suspect.

doc.label: CEFR B1
doc.def: A doctor or medical professional who treats patients and gives medical advice
doc.ex: I need to see the doc about my persistent headaches before they get worse.

dock.label: CEFR B2
dock.def: A platform or structure where ships load and unload cargo or passengers.
dock.ex: The cargo ship pulled into the dock early this morning to deliver imported goods from Asia.

doctor/dr./dr.label: CEFR A1
doctor/dr./dr.def: A person who treats sick or injured people in a hospital or clinic.
doctor/dr./dr.ex: My doctor gave me medicine for my cold.

documentary.label: CEFR B1
documentary.def: A film or television programme that shows real events and people, not a story with actors.
documentary.ex: I watched a fascinating documentary about wildlife in Africa last weekend.

dog.label: CEFR A1
dog.def: A common animal with four legs, fur, and a tail that people keep as a pet.
dog.ex: My dog likes to play in the park every morning.

doll.label: CEFR A1
doll.def: A toy made to look like a person, usually a child or baby.
doll.ex: My daughter plays with her doll every day after school.

dollar.label: CEFR A1
dollar.def: Unit of money used in the United States, Canada, and some other countries.

dolphin.label: CEFR B1
dolphin.def: A large, intelligent marine mammal with a curved fin and a friendly-looking face.
dolphin.ex: We saw a dolphin jumping out of the water during our boat trip along the coast.

donate.label: CEFR B2
donate.def: To give money, goods, or services to help a person or organisation, usually without expecting payment in return.
donate.ex: The charity asked wealthy individuals to donate generously to support their new children's hospital.

donation.label: CEFR B2
donation.def: Money or goods given freely to help a person, organisation, or cause.
donation.ex: The charity received a generous donation from a local business to support their homeless shelter.

donkey.label: CEFR B2
donkey.def: A sturdy hoofed animal similar to a horse but smaller, with long ears and used as a working animal.
donkey.ex: The farmer used a donkey to carry heavy loads up the mountain path.

doom.label: CEFR B2
doom.def: Death, destruction, or a terrible fate that seems certain to happen
doom.ex: The economic report painted a picture of doom for small businesses struggling with rising costs.

door.label: CEFR A1
door.def: A flat object that opens and closes to enter or leave a room or building
door.ex: Please close the door when you come inside.

doorkeeper.label: CEFR B2
doorkeeper.def: A person employed to control access to a building or room and assist visitors who arrive
doorkeeper.ex: The doorkeeper at the museum checked our tickets before allowing us to enter the exhibition.

doorway.label: CEFR B2
doorway.def: An opening in a wall that allows passage between two spaces, typically fitted with a door.
doorway.ex: She stood in the doorway of the conference room, observing the heated debate between the board members before deciding whether to enter.

dorm.label: CEFR B2
dorm.def: A building where students live during their studies at university or college.
dorm.ex: Many first-year students prefer living in a university dorm because it helps them make friends and participate in campus activities.

dormitory.label: CEFR B2
dormitory.def: A large room or building where many people sleep, especially students or workers sharing a space.
dormitory.ex: The university dormitory houses over two hundred students in shared bedrooms.

dose.label: CEFR B2
dose.def: A measured quantity of medicine or drug taken at one time.
dose.ex: The doctor prescribed a dose of antibiotics to be taken twice daily for ten days.

dot.label: CEFR B1
dot.def: A small round mark or spot on a surface.
dot.ex: She put a dot above the letter 'i' in her handwriting.

double.label: CEFR B2
double.def: To increase something to twice its original amount or size.
double.ex: The company plans to double its workforce over the next two years to meet growing demand.

doubly.label: CEFR B2
doubly.def: To twice the degree or extent; in two ways at the same time
doubly.ex: The new policy is doubly important because it saves money and improves safety.

doubt.label: CEFR B2
doubt.def: To feel uncertain about whether something is true or whether you should believe someone
doubt.ex: The investigator began to doubt the witness's account after finding contradictory evidence.

doubtful.label: CEFR B2
doubtful.def: Feeling uncertain or not convinced that something is true or will happen
doubtful.ex: The financial analyst remained doubtful about the company's ability to meet its quarterly targets despite optimistic projections.

dove.label: CEFR B2
dove.def: A small bird with smooth feathers, often white, traditionally symbolising peace and love.
dove.ex: The white dove released at the wedding ceremony flew gracefully into the sky.

down.label: CEFR A1
down.def: Moving or pointing toward the ground or a lower place.
down.ex: The cat jumped down from the table.

downfall.label: CEFR B2
downfall.def: A sudden loss of power, success, or social position; the cause or reason for this failure
downfall.ex: Corruption and poor leadership were the main factors in the company's downfall.

download.label: CEFR A2
download.def: To copy a file or programme from the internet to your computer.
download.ex: I downloaded a new app on my phone yesterday.

downsize.label: CEFR B2
downsize.def: To make something smaller in size or scale, or to reduce a workforce by employing fewer people.
downsize.ex: The company decided to downsize its operations and close several regional offices to cut costs.

downstairs.label: CEFR A2
downstairs.def: On or to a lower floor of a building, below where you are now.
downstairs.ex: My parents are downstairs in the kitchen making breakfast.

downtown.label: CEFR A2
downtown.def: In or towards the central part of a city where many shops and businesses are
downtown.ex: We went downtown to buy clothes at the shopping centre.

downward.label: CEFR B1
downward.def: Moving or pointing towards a lower level or position
downward.ex: The downward trend in house prices has worried many homeowners this year.

dowry.label: CEFR B2
dowry.def: Money or property given by a bride's family to the groom or his family at marriage.
dowry.ex: In historical times, a woman's dowry often determined her prospects for marriage and her family's social status.

doze.label: CEFR B1
doze.def: A short, light sleep that lasts only a few minutes.
doze.ex: I had a quick doze on the train during my commute this morning.

dozen.label: CEFR B1
dozen.ex: I bought a dozen eggs at the supermarket this morning.

draft/draught.label: CEFR B2
draft/draught.def: A current of cool air moving through a room or building, often unintentionally from a window or door.
draft/draught.ex: There's a draught coming from the old window frame, so we've put up heavy curtains to keep warm.

drag.label: CEFR B1
drag.def: To pull something along a surface slowly using force.
drag.ex: She dragged her suitcase across the airport terminal to find her departure gate.

drain.label: CEFR B2
drain.def: To remove liquid from something, or to become gradually empty of liquid or strength.
drain.ex: The government had to drain the flooded areas before rebuilding could begin.

drama.label: CEFR A1
drama.def: A story performed by actors on stage or in a film; a play.
drama.ex: My sister loves performing in drama at school.

dramatic.label: CEFR B1
dramatic.def: Sudden, striking, and very noticeable; causing strong feelings or surprise
dramatic.ex: There has been a dramatic increase in the number of students learning English this year.

dramatically.label: CEFR B2
dramatically.def: In a sudden and very noticeable way; with great intensity or effect.
dramatically.ex: The company's profit margins have dramatically improved over the last two years.

drapery.label: CEFR B2
drapery.def: Cloth arranged in folds for decoration or function, especially fabric hangings on windows or walls.
drapery.ex: The hotel's luxurious drapery in deep blue velvet created an elegant atmosphere in the dining room.

draw.label: CEFR A1
draw.def: To make a picture or design with a pen, pencil, or crayon.
draw.ex: She likes to draw pictures of animals in her notebook.

drawer.label: CEFR A2
drawer.def: A box-shaped container that slides in and out of a desk or table to store things.
drawer.ex: I keep my socks and underwear in the top drawer of my bedroom chest.

drawing.label: CEFR A2
drawing.def: A picture made by hand using pencils, pens, or similar tools.
drawing.ex: She made a beautiful drawing of her family with coloured pencils.

dread.label: CEFR B2
dread.def: To feel anxious fear or worry about something that might happen in the future.
dread.ex: She dreaded the upcoming job interview and couldn't sleep the night before.

dreadful.label: CEFR B2
dreadful.def: Extremely bad, unpleasant, or causing great worry or fear.
dreadful.ex: The dreadful weather conditions forced the organisers to postpone the outdoor concert until next month.

dream.label: CEFR A2
dream.def: To see images and stories in your mind while you sleep.
dream.ex: Last night I dreamed that I was flying over the ocean.

dreamy.label: CEFR B2
dreamy.def: Vague, ethereal, or lost in thought; having a quality of being unreal or imaginative.
dreamy.ex: The musician's dreamy vocals and soft instrumentation created an atmosphere that transported listeners to another world.

dress.label: CEFR A2
dress.def: To put clothes on your body.
dress.ex: I dress quickly in the morning before going to school.

dressed.label: CEFR A2
dressed.def: Wearing clothes; having put on clothes for a particular occasion or activity
dressed.ex: She was dressed in a blue dress and ready for the party.

drill.label: CEFR A2
drill.def: A tool that makes holes in wood, metal, or other hard materials.
drill.ex: Dad used a drill to make a hole in the wall for the picture frame.

drink.label: CEFR A1
drink.def: To take liquid into your mouth and swallow it.

drinkable.label: CEFR B2
drinkable.def: Safe and clean enough to drink without causing harm or illness
drinkable.ex: After the water treatment plant was repaired, the tap water became drinkable again.

drive.label: CEFR A2
drive.def: To operate and control a car or vehicle
drive.ex: My father drives to work every morning in his blue car.

driver.label: CEFR A1
driver.def: A person who operates a car or other vehicle.
driver.ex: My dad is a taxi driver and he works every day.

driver's license/driving licence.label: CEFR A2
driver's license/driving licence.def: An official document that proves you are allowed to drive a car on public roads.
driver's license/driving licence.ex: I passed my driving test last month and got my driving licence.

droop.label: CEFR B2
droop.def: A downward bend or sag in something, usually caused by weight or weakness
droop.ex: The droop in the old wooden fence was finally repaired after the winter storm.

drop.label: CEFR A1
drop.def: To let something fall from your hand or from a height
drop.ex: I dropped my phone on the floor.

dropout.label: CEFR B2
dropout.def: A person who leaves school or a course before completing it.
dropout.ex: The school implemented new support programmes to reduce the dropout rate amongst disadvantaged students.

drought.label: CEFR B2
drought.def: A prolonged period of abnormally low rainfall, leading to a shortage of water.
drought.ex: The severe drought in East Africa has forced thousands of farmers to abandon their land and migrate to cities.

drown.label: CEFR B1
drown.def: To die in water because you cannot breathe
drown.ex: He jumped into the pool but nearly drowned because he couldn't swim well.

drug.label: CEFR A2
drug.def: Medicine you take to treat illness or pain
drug.ex: The doctor prescribed a drug to help with my headaches during my trip.

drugstore.label: CEFR A2
drugstore.def: A shop where you can buy medicines, health products, and everyday items like toiletries.
drugstore.ex: I went to the drugstore to buy toothpaste and cough medicine.

drum.label: CEFR A1
drum.def: A round musical instrument you hit with sticks or hands to make sound.
drum.ex: He plays the drum in the school band.

drunk.label: CEFR B1
drunk.def: Unable to think or move normally because of drinking too much alcohol
drunk.ex: He was so drunk last night that he could barely walk home.

dry.label: CEFR A2
dry.def: To remove water or moisture from something
dry.ex: I dried my hands with a towel after washing them.

duck.label: CEFR A2
duck.def: A water bird with a flat beak, webbed feet, and feathers that keep it dry.
duck.ex: The duck swam across the pond with her ducklings following behind.

dude.label: CEFR B1
dude.def: A man or boy; an informal, casual way to address or refer to someone
dude.ex: Hey dude, do you want to grab a coffee after work?

due.label: CEFR A1
due.def: Expected or supposed to happen at a particular time
due.ex: The bus is due at 3 o'clock.

duel.label: CEFR B2
duel.def: A formal fight between two people to settle a dispute or defend honour.
duel.ex: The famous duel between Alexander Hamilton and Aaron Burr took place in 1804 and resulted in Hamilton's death.

dull.label: CEFR B1
dull.def: Not interesting or exciting; boring or lacking brightness and shine
dull.ex: The conference was dull, so many people left early to find something more engaging.

dumb.label: CEFR B2
dumb.def: Unable to speak, or (informal) lacking intelligence or seeming foolish
dumb.ex: It was a dumb decision to leave his wallet on the bus, but fortunately someone returned it.

dump.label: CEFR B1
dump.def: To put or throw something unwanted in a place, usually carelessly or illegally.
dump.ex: The factory was fined for dumping toxic waste into the river.

duplicate.label: CEFR B2
duplicate.def: An exact copy of something, especially a document or file made for backup or verification purposes.
duplicate.ex: The accountant discovered a duplicate invoice in the system and removed it to prevent double billing.

duplication.label: CEFR B2
duplication.def: The act of making an exact copy of something; something that has been copied unnecessarily.
duplication.ex: The report contained duplication of data that should have been removed before publication.

dust.label: CEFR A2
dust.def: Fine powder particles that settle on surfaces in homes and buildings.
dust.ex: There is dust on the shelf, so I need to clean it with a cloth.

dustbin.label: CEFR B1
dustbin.def: A large container where you put waste and garbage before it is collected
dustbin.ex: I threw the empty pizza box into the dustbin after dinner.

dusty.label: CEFR B1
dusty.ex: After the building work finished, everything in the house was dusty and needed cleaning.

duty.label: CEFR B1
duty.def: A task or responsibility that you must do, especially at work or because of your position.
duty.ex: As a nurse, her main duty is to care for patients in the hospital.

duty-free.label: CEFR B1
duty-free.def: Goods sold without tax, usually at airports or on ships for international travel.
duty-free.ex: She bought perfume at the duty-free shop before her flight to Paris.

duvet.label: CEFR B1
duvet.def: A thick, soft cover filled with feathers or synthetic material, used on top of a bed instead of blankets
duvet.ex: I pulled the duvet up to my chin because it was cold in the bedroom.

dvd.label: CEFR A1
dvd.def: A disc that stores movies, TV shows, or other information you can play on a machine
dvd.ex: I borrowed a DVD of my favourite film from the library yesterday.

dwarf.label: CEFR B2
dwarf.def: A mythical creature depicted as a small person with magical powers, common in fantasy literature and folklore.
dwarf.ex: In Tolkien's novels, the dwarf Gimli becomes a central character whose bravery and loyalty drive much of the narrative.

dye.label: CEFR B2
dye.def: A liquid or powder substance used to change the colour of cloth, hair, or other materials permanently.
dye.ex: She used a natural plant-based dye to colour her wool fabric deep indigo.

dynamic.label: CEFR B2
dynamic.def: Energetic, constantly changing, and full of activity or progress.
dynamic.ex: The dynamic nature of the technology industry means companies must continuously innovate to remain competitive.

dynasty.label: CEFR B1
dynasty.def: A series of rulers from the same family who govern a country for many generations.
dynasty.ex: The Ming Dynasty ruled China for nearly three hundred years and built much of the Great Wall.

eager.label: CEFR B1
eager.def: Wanting something very much; keen and enthusiastic about doing something
eager.ex: She was eager to start her new job and arrived early on her first day.

eagerly.label: CEFR B2
eagerly.def: In a way that shows keen interest or enthusiasm about something.
eagerly.ex: The students eagerly awaited the results of their university entrance exams.

eagerness.label: CEFR B1
eagerness.def: A strong feeling of wanting to do something or wanting something to happen very much.
eagerness.ex: The eagerness in her eyes showed how much she wanted to win the competition.

eagle.label: CEFR B2
eagle.def: A large bird of prey with powerful wings, sharp talons, and excellent eyesight that hunts small animals.
eagle.ex: The golden eagle soared above the mountain peaks, searching for rabbits in the valleys below.

ear.label: CEFR A1
ear.def: The body part on the side of your head that you use to hear sounds
ear.ex: She has a small earring in her left ear.

earache.label: CEFR B1
earache.def: Sharp or dull pain inside the ear, often caused by infection or inflammation.
earache.ex: After swimming yesterday, I developed an earache that made it difficult to concentrate at work.

early.label: CEFR A1
early.def: Before the usual or expected time
early.ex: She arrived early for the class, so she had time to prepare.

earn.label: CEFR A2
earn.def: To receive money for work you do
earn.ex: She earns £2,000 every month working as a teacher.

earnest.label: CEFR B1
earnest.def: Sincere and serious about what you do or say; truly wanting something to happen
earnest.ex: She made an earnest effort to improve her English by studying every day.

earnings.label: CEFR B2
earnings.def: Money that a person or company receives from work or business activities, especially profit or income.
earnings.ex: The technology company's earnings increased by 15% in the last quarter, exceeding investor expectations.

earring.label: CEFR A2
earring.def: A piece of jewellery that you wear on your ear.
earring.ex: She wore a silver earring on each ear.

earth.label: CEFR A2
earth.def: The planet we live on; the world and everything on it.
earth.ex: Many different countries and people live on Earth.

earthquake.label: CEFR A2
earthquake.def: A sudden, violent shaking of the ground caused by movement in the Earth's crust.
earthquake.ex: The earthquake damaged many buildings in the city and made people very frightened.

ease.label: CEFR B2
ease.def: To make something less difficult, painful, or severe; to reduce tension or discomfort
ease.ex: The new policy should ease the burden on small businesses struggling with rising costs.

easily.label: CEFR A2
easily.def: without difficulty or effort; in a way that is not hard
easily.ex: She speaks English easily because she practises every day.

east.label: CEFR A2
east.def: The direction where the sun rises; one of the four main compass directions.
east.ex: The sun comes up in the east every morning.

eastern.label: CEFR B1
eastern.def: Located in or relating to the east part of a place or region.
eastern.ex: The eastern coast of Australia has some beautiful beaches that attract many tourists.

eastward.label: CEFR B2
eastward.def: In the direction of the east; towards the rising sun.
eastward.ex: The river flows eastward through several valleys before reaching the coast.

easy.label: CEFR A1
easy.def: Not difficult; requires little effort or skill to do
easy.ex: This puzzle is easy for my sister because she is very smart.

easygoing/easy-going.label: CEFR B1
easygoing/easy-going.def: Relaxed and calm, not easily annoyed or worried about things
easygoing/easy-going.ex: My easygoing neighbour doesn't mind if the children play music in the garden.

eat.label: CEFR A1
eat.def: To put food in your mouth and swallow it.
eat.ex: I eat breakfast at 7 o'clock every morning.

eco.label: CEFR B1
eco.def: Short form of ecology; the study of living things and their environment.
eco.ex: The eco of the rainforest is changing due to deforestation and climate change.

ecological.label: CEFR B1
ecological.def: Related to the environment and how living things interact with each other and nature.
ecological.ex: The construction of the new road had serious ecological effects on the local wildlife.

ecologically.label: CEFR B2
ecologically.def: In a way that relates to the environment and the relationships between living things and their surroundings
ecologically.ex: The company has committed to sourcing materials ecologically responsibly by reducing its carbon footprint.

ecologist.label: CEFR B2
ecologist.def: A scientist who studies the relationships between living organisms and their environment.
ecologist.ex: The ecologist warned that the destruction of rainforests threatens countless species of plants and animals.

ecology.label: CEFR B1
ecology.def: The study of how organisms and their environment interact and affect each other.
ecology.ex: In our biology class, we learned about ecology and how plants depend on insects for pollination.

economic.label: CEFR B1
economic.def: Related to money, jobs, business, and the production and use of goods and services.
economic.ex: The government announced new economic policies to help small businesses grow and create more jobs.

economical.label: CEFR B2
economical.def: Using money, time, or resources carefully without wasting them; efficient and cost-effective.
economical.ex: Switching to LED bulbs is an economical way to reduce your electricity bills significantly.

economics.label: CEFR B1
economics.def: The study of money, jobs, trade, and how people and countries use resources.
economics.ex: She decided to study economics at university because she wanted to understand how businesses work.

economist.label: CEFR B2
economist.def: A person who studies how money, goods, and services move through society and affect people's lives.
economist.ex: The economist warned that rising inflation could reduce consumer spending over the next year.

ecosystem.label: CEFR B1
ecosystem.def: A community of living things and their environment that work together in nature.
ecosystem.ex: Rainforests are complex ecosystems that contain thousands of different plant and animal species.

ecstasy.label: CEFR B1
ecstasy.def: Intense happiness or joy that makes someone feel extremely excited and emotional
ecstasy.ex: She was in ecstasy when she found out she had won the competition.

ecstatic.label: CEFR B2
ecstatic.def: Feeling extremely happy and excited beyond normal joy or pleasure.
ecstatic.ex: She was ecstatic when she received the acceptance letter from her top choice university.

edge.label: CEFR B1
edge.def: The border or outer line where something ends or meets another surface.
edge.ex: Be careful not to trip on the edge of the carpet.

edit.label: CEFR B2
edit.def: To make changes to a text, film, or other work to improve it or correct mistakes.
edit.ex: The journalist had to edit the article several times before it was ready for publication.

edition.label: CEFR B1
edition.def: A particular version of a book or newspaper, often with a specific publication date or number of copies
edition.ex: The first edition of Harry Potter is now worth a lot of money to collectors.

editor.label: CEFR A2
editor.def: A person who checks and improves written work before it is published.
editor.ex: The editor corrected the mistakes in the newspaper article.

editorial.label: CEFR B2
editorial.def: An article in a newspaper or magazine expressing the publication's opinion on a current issue.
editorial.ex: The newspaper's editorial criticised the government's new housing policy and called for immediate reform.

educate.label: CEFR B1
educate.def: To teach someone, usually in a school or formal setting, to develop their knowledge and skills.
educate.ex: The government has invested in new programmes to educate young people about the dangers of climate change.

educated.label: CEFR B2
educated.def: Having acquired knowledge and skills through formal schooling or study.
educated.ex: She came from an educated family where reading and learning were highly valued.

education.label: CEFR A2
education.def: Learning and teaching at schools or colleges, especially formal study of subjects.
education.ex: My sister is studying hard because she wants a good education and a better job in the future.

educational.label: CEFR A2
educational.def: Related to learning or teaching in schools and other places.
educational.ex: The museum has many educational programs for children.

educator.label: CEFR B2
educator.def: A person who teaches or trains others, typically in schools or professional settings.
educator.ex: The experienced educator developed innovative teaching methods to engage her students in critical thinking.

effect.label: CEFR A2
effect.def: A change or result that happens because of something else
effect.ex: The medicine had a good effect on her health.

effective.label: CEFR B1
effective.def: Successful in producing the wanted result or having a strong impact.
effective.ex: Regular exercise is an effective way to improve your health and fitness.

effectively.label: CEFR B2
effectively.def: In a way that produces the desired result or has a strong impact.
effectively.ex: The new safety measures have effectively reduced workplace accidents by 40 percent.

effectiveness.label: CEFR B2
effectiveness.def: The degree to which something succeeds in producing the intended result.
effectiveness.ex: The effectiveness of the new marketing campaign was measured by the increase in customer engagement.

efficiency.label: CEFR B1
efficiency.def: Working well without wasting time, energy, or materials.
efficiency.ex: The new software improved the efficiency of our office, so we finish projects faster.

efficient.label: CEFR B1
efficient.def: Working well without wasting time, energy, or materials
efficient.ex: An efficient worker completes their tasks quickly and carefully.

effort.label: CEFR A2
effort.def: Hard work or energy you use to do something difficult
effort.ex: She made a great effort to finish her homework on time.

egg.label: CEFR A1
egg.def: A round object with a shell that comes from birds, used as food.
egg.ex: I eat an egg and toast for breakfast every morning.

either.label: CEFR A1
either.def: One or the other of two things or people
either.ex: You can have either tea or coffee with your breakfast.

elbow.label: CEFR B1
elbow.def: The joint in the middle of your arm where it bends between shoulder and wrist.
elbow.ex: She leaned on her elbow while reading the newspaper at the café.

elderly.label: CEFR A2
elderly.def: Old in age; used politely to describe people who are very old.
elderly.ex: My elderly grandmother loves to sit in the garden on sunny days.

elect.label: CEFR B2
elect.def: To choose someone for a position of power by voting in an official process.
elect.ex: The government was elected with a strong mandate to reform the education system.

election.label: CEFR B1
election.def: An official process where people vote to choose a government leader or make an important decision.
election.ex: The election for the new president will be held next month, and millions of citizens are expected to vote.

elective.label: CEFR B2
elective.def: Not required; chosen because you want to do or study it, rather than being obligatory.
elective.ex: Most students choose at least two elective courses in their final year of secondary school.

electric.label: CEFR A2
electric.def: Using or powered by electricity; needing electrical power to work
electric.ex: My electric kettle boils water very quickly in the morning.

electrical.label: CEFR B1
electrical.def: Related to or using electricity as a power source
electrical.ex: The electrical system in the house needs to be checked by a professional before we move in.

electrician.label: CEFR B2
electrician.def: A qualified professional who installs, repairs, and maintains electrical systems in buildings and equipment.
electrician.ex: We called an electrician to fix the faulty wiring in our kitchen after the circuit breaker kept tripping.

electricity.label: CEFR B1
electricity.def: Power that flows through wires to make lights, machines, and devices work.
electricity.ex: The train runs on electricity from overhead cables.

electron.label: CEFR B1
electron.def: A tiny particle of matter with negative electrical charge that moves around an atom's nucleus.
electron.ex: The movement of electrons through a wire creates electrical current that powers your phone.

electronic.label: CEFR B1
electronic.def: Operated by or involving electricity and computer technology.
electronic.ex: She prefers to read electronic books on her tablet rather than printed books.

electronically.label: CEFR B2
electronically.def: By using electronic devices or systems to perform, transmit, or process information.
electronically.ex: The company now processes all invoices electronically rather than using paper.

electronics.label: CEFR B2
electronics.def: Devices and systems that use electrical current to control information, power, or signals.
electronics.ex: The smartphone's electronics were damaged when it fell into water, so she had to send it for repairs.

elegance.label: CEFR B2
elegance.def: The quality of being stylish, refined, and gracefully simple in appearance or manner.
elegance.ex: The elegance of her minimalist design impressed everyone at the architecture exhibition.

elegant.label: CEFR B2
elegant.def: Graceful and stylish in appearance or manner, showing refined taste and simplicity.
elegant.ex: The architect designed an elegant solution to the structural problem that impressed the entire team.

elegantly.label: CEFR B2
elegantly.def: In a graceful, stylish manner showing good taste and refinement.
elegantly.ex: She elegantly solved the complex problem using a surprisingly simple mathematical approach.

elemental.label: CEFR B2
elemental.def: Relating to the most basic or essential parts of something; simple and fundamental.
elemental.ex: The elemental forces of nature—wind, water, and fire—have shaped Earth's landscapes for millions of years.

elementary.label: CEFR A1
elementary.def: Very simple or basic; at the earliest level of learning.
elementary.ex: She goes to elementary school with children aged five to eleven.

elephant.label: CEFR A2
elephant.def: A very large grey animal with a long trunk, big ears, and thick legs that lives in Africa or Asia.
elephant.ex: The elephant sprayed water on its back with its trunk to stay cool.

elevator.label: CEFR A2
elevator.def: A machine that moves people up and down between different floors of a building.
elevator.ex: I took the elevator to the third floor of the shopping centre.

eligible.label: CEFR B2
eligible.def: Having the right qualifications or meeting the necessary requirements for something.
eligible.ex: Students with a grade of B or higher are eligible for the scholarship programme.

eliminate.label: CEFR B1
eliminate.def: To remove or get rid of something completely.
eliminate.ex: The new software helped eliminate errors in the accounting department.

elite.label: CEFR B2
elite.def: A small group of people with the most power, wealth, or influence in society.
elite.ex: The country's political elite gathered at the conference to discuss economic policy.

eloquence.label: CEFR B2
eloquence.def: The ability to speak or write in a persuasive and expressive way that moves or influences people.
eloquence.ex: The lawyer's eloquence during the closing argument swayed the jury in favour of her client.

eloquent.label: Oxford 5000 · C1
eloquent.def: Able to speak or write in a persuasive and expressive way that captures attention.
eloquent.ex: The politician's eloquent speech about climate change moved many voters to reconsider their environmental policies.

else.label: CEFR A1
else.ex: Hurry up, or else you will be late for school.

elsewhere.label: CEFR A2
elsewhere.def: In or to another place that is not here
elsewhere.ex: I couldn't find my keys at home, so they must be elsewhere.

email.label: CEFR A2
email.def: To send a message using the internet to another person's computer or phone.
email.ex: I emailed my teacher to ask about the homework assignment.

email/e-mail/e-mail.label: CEFR A1
email/e-mail/e-mail.def: A message sent to someone using a computer or phone through the internet
email/e-mail/e-mail.ex: I sent you an email with the meeting details this morning.

embarrass.label: CEFR B1
embarrass.def: To make someone feel shy, awkward, or uncomfortable in front of others.
embarrass.ex: She didn't want to embarrass herself by asking a stupid question in the meeting.

embarrassed.label: CEFR B1
embarrassed.def: Feeling self-conscious or ashamed because of something you did or that happened to you.
embarrassed.ex: She felt embarrassed when she tripped and fell in front of all her classmates.

embarrassing.label: CEFR A2
embarrassing.def: Making you feel shy, ashamed, or uncomfortable in front of other people.
embarrassing.ex: I fell down the stairs at school—it was so embarrassing!

embarrassment.label: CEFR B1
embarrassment.def: The uncomfortable feeling you have when you think people are judging you badly.
embarrassment.ex: She felt embarrassment when she tripped and fell in front of her classmates.

embassy.label: CEFR B2
embassy.def: A government building in a foreign country that represents and protects its nation's interests and citizens.
embassy.ex: She works at the British embassy in Paris, helping citizens with passport applications and visa issues.

embrace.label: CEFR B2
embrace.def: A close hold of someone with your arms, usually as a sign of affection or greeting.
embrace.ex: She ran to her father and gave him a warm embrace after months apart.

emergence.label: CEFR B2
emergence.def: The process of becoming visible, known, or prominent after being hidden or developing gradually
emergence.ex: The emergence of social media has fundamentally changed how people communicate with each other.

emergency.label: CEFR A2
emergency.def: A serious, dangerous situation that needs immediate help or action
emergency.ex: Call the police immediately if there is an emergency in your area.

emotion.label: CEFR B1
emotion.def: A strong feeling such as happiness, sadness, anger, or fear.
emotion.ex: She couldn't hide her emotion when she heard the news about winning the competition.

emotional.label: CEFR B1
emotional.def: Involving or showing strong feelings like happiness, sadness, or anger.
emotional.ex: The emotional scene in the film made many people in the cinema cry.

emotionally.label: CEFR B1
emotionally.def: In a way that shows or involves deep feelings and strong reactions.
emotionally.ex: She spoke emotionally about losing her grandmother, and many people in the audience cried.

emperor.label: CEFR B1
emperor.def: A male ruler who governs an empire or multiple countries and their territories.
emperor.ex: The Roman emperor Augustus ruled over one of history's greatest empires.

emphasize/emphasize.label: CEFR B1
emphasize/emphasize.def: To give special importance or attention to something when speaking or writing.
emphasize/emphasize.ex: The teacher emphasised the importance of studying regularly for the exam.

emphatically.label: CEFR B2
emphatically.def: In a forceful way that shows strong feeling or importance about something.
emphatically.ex: The minister emphatically denied the accusations during the press conference yesterday.

empire.label: CEFR B1
empire.def: A large group of countries or territories controlled by one powerful nation or ruler.
empire.ex: The Roman Empire was one of the most powerful empires in history, spanning three continents.

employ.label: CEFR B2
employ.def: To hire someone to work for you or your organisation, typically for regular payment
employ.ex: The manufacturing company employs over 500 workers across its three factories in the region.

employee.label: CEFR B2
employee.def: A person who works for a company or organisation and receives regular payment for their work.
employee.ex: The company announced that each employee would receive a bonus at the end of the financial year.

employer.label: CEFR B2
employer.def: A person or organisation that pays workers to do a job for them.
employer.ex: Her employer offered her a promotion after three years of excellent performance.

employment.label: CEFR B1
employment.def: A job or position where someone works and earns money for a company or employer.
employment.ex: She found employment as a teacher at a local secondary school.

empty.label: CEFR A2
empty.def: Having nothing inside; not containing anything.
empty.ex: The bottle is empty, so I need to buy a new one.

enclose.label: CEFR B1
enclose.def: To surround or wrap something completely so it is contained or protected.
enclose.ex: The garden is enclosed by a high wooden fence to keep the animals in.

encounter.label: CEFR B2
encounter.def: To meet or experience something, usually unexpectedly or by chance.
encounter.ex: While researching climate change, scientists encountered new data suggesting the problem was more severe than previously thought.

encourage.label: CEFR A2
encourage.def: To give someone confidence or support to do something
encourage.ex: My teacher encouraged me to try the difficult maths problem.

encouragement.label: CEFR B1
encouragement.def: Words or actions that give someone confidence and make them want to continue trying
encouragement.ex: With encouragement from his coach, Marcus trained harder and finally won the competition.

encouraging.label: CEFR B1
encouraging.def: Giving hope or confidence that something will succeed or improve
encouraging.ex: The encouraging results from the first test made the students feel more confident about the exam.

encyclopedia/encyclopaedia.label: CEFR B1
encyclopedia/encyclopaedia.def: A book or website containing facts and information about many different subjects arranged in alphabetical order.
encyclopedia/encyclopaedia.ex: I found detailed information about ancient Egypt in the encyclopedia at the library.

end.label: CEFR A2
end.def: To finish or stop something happening

endanger.label: CEFR B1
endanger.def: To put someone or something in a situation where they could be harmed or damaged.
endanger.ex: Driving too fast endangers the lives of other road users.

endangered.label: CEFR A2
endangered.def: In danger of disappearing or dying out completely.
endangered.ex: Giant pandas are an endangered species, but there are now more of them in the wild.

endeavor/endeavour.label: CEFR B2
endeavor/endeavour.def: A serious, sustained attempt to achieve something difficult or important.
endeavor/endeavour.ex: The team's endeavour to develop a sustainable energy solution has attracted significant investment from major corporations.

ending.label: CEFR A2
ending.def: The last part or final moment of something
ending.ex: I cried at the ending of the sad film.

endless.label: CEFR B1
endless.def: Continuing for a very long time without stopping or finishing
endless.ex: The endless traffic on the motorway made us arrive late to the meeting.

endlessly.label: CEFR B2
endlessly.def: For a very long time without stopping or seeming to have an end.
endlessly.ex: The debate about climate change policy seemed to continue endlessly, with both sides refusing to compromise.

endorse.label: Oxford 5000 · C1
endorse.def: To support or approve something publicly; to state that something is good or correct.
endorse.ex: The health organisation endorsed the new vaccine after extensive testing proved its safety.

endurance.label: CEFR B2
endurance.def: The ability to sustain physical or mental effort over a long period without giving up.
endurance.ex: The marathon runner's exceptional endurance allowed her to maintain a steady pace for over four hours.

endure.label: CEFR B1
endure.def: To survive or experience something difficult without giving up or complaining
endure.ex: The athletes endured hours of training to prepare for the Olympic Games.

enduring.label: CEFR B2
enduring.def: Lasting a long time; continuing to exist or remain relevant despite difficulties.
enduring.ex: The enduring popularity of Shakespeare's plays shows their timeless appeal to audiences worldwide.

enemy.label: CEFR B1
enemy.def: A person or group that opposes you and wants to harm you.
enemy.ex: During the war, the two countries became enemies and fought for many years.

energetic.label: CEFR A2
energetic.def: Full of energy and enthusiasm; active and lively.
energetic.ex: She is very energetic in the morning and likes to run before breakfast.

engage.label: CEFR B1
engage.def: To become involved in an activity or to capture someone's attention and interest.
engage.ex: The teacher uses games to engage students in learning English vocabulary.

engaged.label: CEFR B1
engaged.def: Interested and involved in something, or married or having promised to marry someone.
engaged.ex: She was so engaged in her work that she didn't notice the time pass.

engagement.label: CEFR B2
engagement.def: The state of being involved in or interested in something; the process of becoming involved with.
engagement.ex: The teacher noticed improved student engagement after introducing interactive discussions in the classroom.

engine.label: CEFR B1
engine.def: A machine that uses fuel or electricity to produce power and make things move or work.
engine.ex: The car's engine started making strange noises, so I took it to the mechanic.

engineer.label: CEFR A1
engineer.def: A person who designs and builds machines, roads, or buildings.
engineer.ex: My father is an engineer who works on building bridges.

engineering.label: CEFR B1
engineering.def: The practical application of science and mathematics to design and build structures, machines, and systems.
engineering.ex: She studied engineering at university because she wanted to design bridges and buildings.

englishman.label: CEFR B1
englishman.def: A man who was born in or is a citizen of England.
englishman.ex: The Englishman ordered a cup of tea at the café in Paris.

engulf.label: CEFR B2
engulf.def: To completely surround or swallow up something, often suddenly and completely.
engulf.ex: The small fishing village was engulfed by flames when the wildfire spread rapidly across the region.

enjoy.label: CEFR A1
enjoy.def: To get pleasure or happiness from something
enjoy.ex: I enjoy playing football with my friends on weekends.

enjoyable.label: CEFR B1
enjoyable.def: Giving pleasure or satisfaction; making you feel happy.
enjoyable.ex: The film was very enjoyable, and we all laughed together throughout.

enjoyment.label: CEFR B1
enjoyment.def: The feeling of pleasure or happiness that you get from something
enjoyment.ex: She got great enjoyment from reading novels on holiday.

enough.label: CEFR A2
enough.def: As much as needed; sufficient in amount or degree.
enough.ex: I have enough money to buy a coffee.

enrich.label: CEFR B1
enrich.def: To make something better or more interesting by adding valuable things to it.
enrich.ex: Travelling to different countries can enrich your understanding of other cultures.

enroll/enrol.label: CEFR B2
enroll/enrol.def: To register officially as a student or member of an institution or programme
enroll/enrol.ex: She decided to enrol in a master's degree programme at Cambridge University starting next September.

enter.label: CEFR A2
enter.def: To go or come into a place or building
enter.ex: She entered the classroom and sat at her desk.

enterprise.label: CEFR B2
enterprise.def: A business venture or company, or a bold ambitious project requiring initiative and effort.
enterprise.ex: The startup's enterprise in renewable energy has attracted significant investment from venture capitalists.

entertain.label: CEFR B1
entertain.def: To provide amusement or enjoyment to someone.
entertain.ex: The magician entertained the children with clever card tricks at the birthday party.

entertainer.label: CEFR B1
entertainer.def: A person whose job is to make people laugh, dance, or enjoy themselves through performances
entertainer.ex: The entertainer juggled colourful balls and told funny jokes to make the children laugh at the birthday party.

entertaining.label: CEFR B2
entertaining.def: Enjoyable and interesting; holds your attention and makes you happy
entertaining.ex: The documentary provided an entertaining look at the history of space exploration, combining stunning visuals with compelling storytelling.

entertainment.label: CEFR A2
entertainment.def: Activities or performances that people enjoy for fun and pleasure
entertainment.ex: Going to the cinema is my favourite entertainment on Friday nights.

enthusiasm.label: CEFR B1
enthusiasm.def: Strong feeling of excitement and interest in something
enthusiasm.ex: Her enthusiasm for learning new languages impressed all her teachers.

enthusiast.label: CEFR B1
enthusiast.def: A person who is very interested in and excited about a particular activity or subject.
enthusiast.ex: My brother is a football enthusiast who watches every match and collects old programmes.

enthusiastic.label: CEFR B1
enthusiastic.def: Very excited and interested in something; showing a lot of energy and positive feeling
enthusiastic.ex: She gave an enthusiastic presentation about her research project to the whole class.

enthusiastically.label: CEFR B2
enthusiastically.def: In a way that shows excited interest and energy about something.
enthusiastically.ex: The audience applauded enthusiastically when the band finished their final song.

entire.label: CEFR B1
entire.def: Whole, with nothing left out or removed.
entire.ex: She spent the entire day studying for her exam.

entirely.label: CEFR B1
entirely.def: Completely; with nothing left out or remaining
entirely.ex: The old building was entirely destroyed in the fire, so they had to rebuild it from scratch.

entitle.label: CEFR B2
entitle.def: To give someone the legal right or official permission to have or do something.
entitle.ex: Your degree entitles you to apply for positions that require higher education qualifications.

entrance.label: CEFR A2
entrance.def: A way in to a building or room; a door or opening where you go inside.
entrance.ex: The entrance to the school is on the main street.

entrepreneur.label: CEFR B2
entrepreneur.def: A person who starts and runs their own business, taking on financial risk and responsibility.
entrepreneur.ex: She became a successful entrepreneur after launching her own tech startup five years ago.

entry.label: CEFR B1
entry.def: A way in or a place where you go into a building or area
entry.ex: The main entry to the museum is through the large glass doors on the street.

envelope.label: CEFR A2
envelope.def: A thin paper cover for a letter that you seal and address before posting.
envelope.ex: She put the letter in an envelope and wrote the address on it.

environmental.label: CEFR B1
environmental.def: Related to the natural world, including air, water, soil, and living things around us.
environmental.ex: The company made environmental changes to reduce plastic waste in their factories.

environmentalist.label: CEFR B1
environmentalist.def: A person who works to protect nature and reduce damage to the environment.
environmentalist.ex: The environmentalist gave a presentation about recycling and reducing plastic waste at our school.

environmentally.label: CEFR B2
environmentally.def: In a way that relates to or affects the natural world and living things.
environmentally.ex: The company has committed to environmentally sustainable practices to reduce its carbon footprint.

environmentally friendly.label: CEFR B2
environmentally friendly.def: Designed or produced to cause minimal harm to nature and ecosystems.
environmentally friendly.ex: Many companies now offer environmentally friendly packaging made from recycled materials.

envy.label: CEFR B2
envy.def: To feel a desire to have something that belongs to another person.
envy.ex: She envied her colleague's promotion and wondered why she hadn't been chosen instead.

episode.label: CEFR A2
episode.def: One separate part of a story in a book, TV show, or series
episode.ex: I watched the first episode of the new series last night and loved it.

equal.label: CEFR B2
equal.def: To be the same in value, size, amount, or degree as something else.
equal.ex: The investment returns this year equalled those of the previous decade.

equality.label: CEFR B1
equality.def: The state of having the same rights, opportunities, and treatment as everyone else.
equality.ex: The country's laws now guarantee equality between men and women in the workplace.

equally.label: CEFR B1
equally.def: In the same way or to the same degree as something else.
equally.ex: Both students worked equally hard on the project and received the same grade.

equation.label: CEFR B2
equation.def: A mathematical statement showing that two expressions have the same value, using an equals sign.
equation.ex: To solve the equation 2x + 5 = 13, you need to find the value of x that makes both sides equal.

equip.label: CEFR B2
equip.def: To provide someone with the necessary tools, skills, or resources needed for a task or situation.
equip.ex: The training programme equipped graduates with the technical knowledge required for modern engineering roles.

equipment.label: CEFR B1
equipment.def: Tools, machines, and other items needed to do a particular job or activity.
equipment.ex: The gym has new fitness equipment, including treadmills and weight machines.

equity.label: CEFR B2
equity.def: The quality of being fair and treating everyone equally; also, ownership stake in a company or property value after debts are paid.
equity.ex: The government is committed to promoting equity in education so that all students have equal opportunities to succeed.

era.label: CEFR B1
era.def: A long period of time in history marked by particular events or characteristics.
era.ex: The Victorian era was a time of great industrial growth and social change in Britain.

erect.label: CEFR B2
erect.def: Standing straight and upright; not bent or leaning
erect.ex: The soldier maintained an erect posture throughout the formal ceremony.

erupt.label: CEFR B2
erupt.def: To burst forth suddenly and violently, as when a volcano releases lava and ash explosively
erupt.ex: When the volcano erupted in 1883, the explosion was so powerful it could be heard thousands of kilometres away.

eruption.label: CEFR B2
eruption.def: A sudden, violent outburst of something, especially lava and ash from a volcano or violent emotion
eruption.ex: The volcanic eruption forced thousands of residents to evacuate their homes as ash blanketed the surrounding villages.

escalator.label: CEFR A2
escalator.def: A moving staircase that carries people up or down between different levels.
escalator.ex: I took the escalator to the second floor of the shopping centre.

escape.label: CEFR B1
escape.def: To get away from a place or situation, usually when it is difficult or dangerous.
escape.ex: The prisoner managed to escape from the jail by hiding in a delivery truck.

especially.label: CEFR A2
especially.def: more than usual; to a greater degree; particularly
especially.ex: I like ice cream, especially chocolate ice cream.

essay.label: CEFR A2
essay.def: A short piece of writing about one topic, usually for school or university.
essay.ex: I wrote an essay about climate change for my English class.

essayist.label: CEFR B2
essayist.def: A writer who composes short pieces of non-fiction expressing personal opinions or analysis on specific topics.
essayist.ex: The essayist argued in her latest publication that remote work has fundamentally changed workplace culture.

essence.label: CEFR B1
essence.def: The most important or basic part of something that makes it what it is
essence.ex: The essence of good friendship is trust and honesty between people.

essential.label: CEFR B1
essential.def: Absolutely necessary; something you cannot do without or cannot succeed without
essential.ex: Good communication skills are essential for working in a team environment.

essentially.label: CEFR B2
essentially.def: In the most basic or important way; fundamentally, though perhaps not in every detail
essentially.ex: The two proposals are essentially the same, despite their different wording.

establishment.label: CEFR B1
establishment.def: An organization, business, or institution that has been created and is operating
establishment.ex: The new restaurant establishment on the high street has become very popular with local families.

estate.label: CEFR B2
estate.def: A large area of land in the countryside, typically with a big house and farms or woods.
estate.ex: The wealthy family owned a sprawling estate in the Cotswolds with over 500 acres of gardens and parkland.

estimated.label: CEFR B2
estimated.def: Calculated or judged as approximately correct based on available information.
estimated.ex: The estimated cost of the project is around £50,000, though the final figure may vary.

eternal.label: CEFR B1
eternal.def: Lasting forever; never ending or changing
eternal.ex: Many people believe in eternal life after death.

eternally.label: CEFR B2
eternally.def: For all time; without beginning or end; lasting forever or seeming to last forever
eternally.ex: She felt eternally grateful to the doctor who saved her life.

eternity.label: CEFR B1
eternity.def: Time without beginning or end; forever and always
eternity.ex: Some people believe the soul lives for eternity after death.

ethic.label: CEFR B2
ethic.def: A set of moral principles guiding what is right and wrong in conduct and decision-making.
ethic.ex: The company's strong work ethic and commitment to sustainability earned it international recognition.

euro.label: CEFR A2
euro.def: The money used by many European countries.
euro.ex: A coffee costs about three euros in Italy.

evacuate.label: CEFR B2
evacuate.def: To leave a place quickly because of danger or an emergency situation.
evacuate.ex: When the fire alarm sounded, all employees were instructed to evacuate the building immediately.

evacuation.label: CEFR B2
evacuation.def: The process of removing people quickly from a dangerous place to safety.
evacuation.ex: The evacuation of the building took only fifteen minutes after the fire alarm sounded.

evaluation.label: CEFR B2
evaluation.def: A careful judgement of how good, effective, or valuable something is.
evaluation.ex: The teacher's evaluation of the student's essay focused on both strengths and areas for improvement.

even.label: CEFR A2
even.def: Used to show something is surprising or more than expected.
even.ex: She is very busy, but she even helps her friends with homework.

evening.label: CEFR A1
evening.def: The time of day after afternoon and before night.
evening.ex: I like to walk in the park in the evening after work.

evenly.label: CEFR B1
evenly.def: In a way that is equal or fair in distribution; without variation.
evenly.ex: The teacher divided the chocolates evenly among all the students in the class.

event.label: CEFR A1
event.def: Something that happens, especially something planned or important.
event.ex: There is a football event at the stadium next Saturday.

eventually.label: CEFR B1
eventually.def: At some point in the future, after a long time or after many events
eventually.ex: She studied hard for months, and eventually she passed her driving test.

ever.label: CEFR A2
ever.def: At any time, including now or in the past
ever.ex: Have you ever been to London?

everlasting.label: CEFR B2
everlasting.def: Continuing forever without ever ending or stopping.
everlasting.ex: The couple exchanged everlasting vows of love on their wedding day.

everyday.label: CEFR A1
everyday.def: Normal and usual, happening every day or all the time
everyday.ex: I wear my everyday clothes to school, not special clothes.

everywhere.label: CEFR A1
everywhere.def: In every place or location; all around.
everywhere.ex: There are shops everywhere in the city centre.

evidence.label: CEFR A2
evidence.def: Facts or information that prove something is true or that something happened.
evidence.ex: The police found evidence at the crime scene that helped them catch the criminal.

evidently.label: CEFR B2
evidently.def: Clearly or obviously, based on available information or signs
evidently.ex: The project was evidently successful, as the team received praise from senior management.

evil.label: CEFR B2
evil.def: Profound moral wrongness or wickedness; the force or principle opposing good
evil.ex: The documentary examined how ordinary people can be drawn into committing evil when they follow authoritarian leaders without question.

evolution.label: CEFR B2
evolution.def: The gradual process of change and development of living things over millions of years.
evolution.ex: Darwin's theory of evolution explains how species adapt and change over long periods of time.

exact.label: CEFR B1
exact.def: Correct in every detail; not approximate or estimated.
exact.ex: The exact time of the meeting is 2:30 PM, not 2:45 PM.

exactly.label: CEFR A2
exactly.def: in a way that is correct and precise, with no mistakes or differences
exactly.ex: The train arrived at exactly 3 o'clock.

exactness.label: CEFR B2
exactness.def: The quality of being precise and accurate in every detail without any errors or approximations.
exactness.ex: The surgeon's exactness during the operation ensured the best possible outcome for the patient.

exaggerate.label: CEFR B2
exaggerate.def: The act of describing something as more important, larger, or more serious than it really is.
exaggerate.ex: The exaggeration in his account of the accident made it difficult for the insurance company to assess what actually happened.

exaggeration.label: CEFR B2
exaggeration.def: Representing something as greater or more important than it actually is.
exaggeration.ex: Saying the film was the worst movie ever made is an exaggeration; it was just mediocre.

exalt.label: CEFR B2
exalt.def: To praise someone or something highly or treat them with great respect and admiration.
exalt.ex: The media exalted the athlete's achievements after she won the Olympic gold medal.

exam.label: CEFR A2
exam.def: A test to check if you know something or can do something.
exam.ex: I studied hard for my English exam last week.

examination.label: CEFR B1
examination.def: A formal test to check how much knowledge or skill someone has in a subject.
examination.ex: She spent two weeks studying hard before her final examination in mathematics.

examine.label: CEFR B1
examine.def: To look at something carefully and closely to understand or check it.
examine.ex: The doctor will examine you to make sure you are healthy.

examinee.label: CEFR B2
examinee.def: A person who takes an exam or test to demonstrate their knowledge or skills.
examinee.ex: Each examinee was given two hours to complete the written examination.

examiner.label: CEFR B1
examiner.def: A person who tests or checks someone's knowledge or skills in a particular subject.
examiner.ex: The examiner asked me several difficult questions during my driving test.

example.label: CEFR A1
example.def: Something you can look at to understand how to do something or what something means.
example.ex: My teacher gave me an example of how to write a good sentence.

excel.label: CEFR B2
excel.def: To perform better than others or to be extremely good at something.
excel.ex: She excels in mathematics and has won several national competitions.

excellence.label: CEFR B1
excellence.def: The quality of being extremely good at something; the highest standard of quality.
excellence.ex: The chef's excellence in cooking earned her restaurant a Michelin star.

excellent.label: CEFR A1
excellent.def: Very good; of the highest quality.
excellent.ex: She got an excellent mark on her maths test.

exception.label: CEFR B2
exception.def: A person or thing that does not follow the usual rule or pattern.
exception.ex: Most students enjoyed the new curriculum, but there were a few exceptions who preferred the old system.

exceptional.label: CEFR B2
exceptional.def: Unusually good or remarkable; much better than what is normal or expected.
exceptional.ex: The student's exceptional performance in mathematics earned her a scholarship to university.

exceptionally.label: CEFR B2
exceptionally.def: In a way that is notably unusual, outstanding, or better than typical.
exceptionally.ex: The report was exceptionally well-written, earning praise from all the senior managers.

excess.label: CEFR B1
excess.def: More of something than is needed or wanted.
excess.ex: The excess of sugar in processed foods can cause health problems.

excessive.label: CEFR B2
excessive.def: More than necessary or reasonable; beyond what is acceptable or normal.
excessive.ex: The government warned that excessive alcohol consumption increases the risk of serious health problems.

exchange.label: CEFR B1
exchange.def: To give something and receive something else in return, usually of equal value.
exchange.ex: I exchanged my old laptop for a newer model at the electronics shop.

exchange rate.label: CEFR B1
exchange rate.def: The price of one country's money compared to another country's money.
exchange rate.ex: When I travelled to Japan, I checked the exchange rate to see how many yen I would get for my pounds.

excited.label: CEFR A1
excited.def: Very happy and enthusiastic about something that will happen soon
excited.ex: The children were excited about going to the beach tomorrow.

excitedly.label: CEFR B1
excitedly.def: In a way that shows you feel very happy or enthusiastic about something.
excitedly.ex: The children excitedly opened their presents on Christmas morning.

excitement.label: CEFR B1
excitement.def: A strong feeling of happy enthusiasm and interest about something.
excitement.ex: The excitement in the stadium was incredible when the home team scored the winning goal.

exciting.label: CEFR A1
exciting.def: Making you feel very happy and interested; full of energy and fun
exciting.ex: The exciting football match made all the children jump and cheer.

exclaim.label: CEFR B2
exclaim.def: To say something suddenly and with strong emotion or surprise.
exclaim.ex: When she saw the results, she exclaimed, "I can't believe we won the competition!"

exclamation mark.label: CEFR B2
exclamation mark.def: A punctuation mark (!) used to show strong emotion, emphasis, or a sudden outburst in writing
exclamation mark.ex: She wrote 'I can't believe we won!' with three exclamation marks to show her excitement.

exclusion.label: CEFR B2
exclusion.def: The act of preventing someone from entering a place or participating in an activity or group.
exclusion.ex: The school's exclusion policy meant that students who broke serious rules would be banned from campus for weeks.

exclusive.label: CEFR B1
exclusive.def: Limited to only one person or group; not shared with others.
exclusive.ex: This exclusive club only allows members who have been specially invited.

exclusively.label: CEFR B2
exclusively.def: Only for one person, group, or purpose; not shared with others
exclusively.ex: This luxury resort is exclusively for members who pay the highest membership fee.

excursion.label: CEFR B2
excursion.def: A short journey or trip taken for pleasure, education, or a specific purpose, usually returning the same day.
excursion.ex: The school organised an excursion to the museum so students could learn about ancient Egyptian history firsthand.

excuse.label: CEFR B1
excuse.def: To forgive someone or accept a reason why someone did not do something.
excuse.ex: Please excuse me for being late; my train was delayed.

execute.label: CEFR B2
execute.def: To carry out or complete a plan, order, or piece of work as intended.
execute.ex: The project manager executed the strategy perfectly, and the campaign exceeded all expectations.

execution.label: CEFR B2
execution.def: The carrying out of a plan, order, or process; putting something into action.
execution.ex: The project's success depended heavily on the quality of its execution rather than the originality of the idea itself.

executive.label: CEFR B2
executive.def: A senior manager or official responsible for making important decisions and running an organisation.
executive.ex: The company's new executive decided to implement more flexible working arrangements for all staff members.

exercise.label: CEFR A1
exercise.def: To do physical activity to stay healthy and strong.
exercise.ex: I exercise at the gym three times a week.
